content:
First one checks that in any valid splitting all “blocks” have length at most 3 (otherwise their value exceeds 1000), so if we let

a = #(blocks of length 1),  
b = #(blocks of length 2),  
c = #(blocks of length 3),

then the sum‐of‐values condition becomes

  a·1 + b·11 + c·111 = 1000,

and the total number of 1’s is

  n = a·1 + b·2 + c·3.

From a+11b+111c=1000 we get a=1000−11b−111c, hence

  n = (1000−11b−111c) + 2b + 3c
    = 1000 − 9b −108c.

We must have a≥0, so 11b+111c ≤1000 ⇒ c=0,…,9, and for each c, 0≤b≤⌊(1000−111c)/11⌋.  Writing

  S = b + 12c,

one finds that as c runs 0…9 and b runs 0…⌊(1000−111c)/11⌋, the possible S form all integers 0,1,…,106 and also 108 (but never 107).  Since n=1000−9S this gives exactly 107+1=108 distinct values of n.  Hence a=108 and the sum of its digits is

  1+0+8=9.

\boxed{9}

message.content:
First one checks that in any valid splitting all “blocks” have length at most 3 (otherwise their value exceeds 1000), so if we let

a = #(blocks of length 1),  
b = #(blocks of length 2),  
c = #(blocks of length 3),

then the sum‐of‐values condition becomes

  a·1 + b·11 + c·111 = 1000,

and the total number of 1’s is

  n = a·1 + b·2 + c·3.

From a+11b+111c=1000 we get a=1000−11b−111c, hence

  n = (1000−11b−111c) + 2b + 3c
    = 1000 − 9b −108c.

We must have a≥0, so 11b+111c ≤1000 ⇒ c=0,…,9, and for each c, 0≤b≤⌊(1000−111c)/11⌋.  Writing

  S = b + 12c,

one finds that as c runs 0…9 and b runs 0…⌊(1000−111c)/11⌋, the possible S form all integers 0,1,…,106 and also 108 (but never 107).  Since n=1000−9S this gives exactly 107+1=108 distinct values of n.  Hence a=108 and the sum of its digits is

  1+0+8=9.

\boxed{9}
